informal: Suppose $V$ is a real vector space and $T \in \mathcal{L}(V)$ has no eigenvalues. Prove that every subspace of $V$ invariant under $T$ has even dimension.
reasoning: The informal statement is from Axler, where V is implicitly finite-dimensional (the book's standing assumption when speaking of L(V) and eigenvalues), so the `FiniteDimensional ℝ V` hypothesis matches. "T has no eigenvalues" over ℝ is correctly encoded as `∀ c : ℝ, eigenspace T c = ⊥`. The conclusion `Even (finrank ℝ U)` is the right rendering of "even dimension." The only subtle point is that T-invariance is encoded as `Submodule.map T U = U` (equality) rather than ⊆; however, since T has no eigenvalue 0 it is injective, and on a finite-dimensional invariant subspace an injective endomorphism is surjective, so T(U) ⊆ U is equivalent to T(U) = U under these hypotheses. Hence the Lean statement is logically equivalent to the informal claim.
